title: Algorithms: Sequence, Selection, Iteration
obj: Students will be able to define an algorithm, identify its three fundamental building blocks, and express an algorithm as a flowchart or pseudocode.
content: What is an algorithm
a finite, ordered sequence of steps that transforms input into output — recipes and driving directions qualify
Sequence
steps execute one after another in order; the default control flow
Selection
if/else branching — the algorithm chooses different steps based on a condition
Iteration
loops — repeat steps while a condition holds or for a fixed count
Flowcharts and pseudocode
oval = start/end, rectangle = process, diamond = decision; pseudocode is informal code-like English
Everyday algorithms
brushing teeth, making a sandwich, sorting a deck of cards — same building blocks, different domain
assess: Write an algorithm in pseudocode for making tea, using sequence + selection + iteration
Draw a flowchart for logging into a website with 3 attempts allowed
Short answer: why are these three building blocks sufficient for any algorithm?

title: Designing, Modifying, and Evaluating Algorithms
obj: Students will be able to modify an existing algorithm, create an algorithm for a new problem, and evaluate algorithms analytically and empirically.
content: Modifying an algorithm
change a step, reorder steps, add a branch — test that behavior still matches the goal
Creating an algorithm from a problem
restate the problem; list inputs/outputs; break into steps; test on examples
Analytical evaluation
does it always produce the correct output? does it always terminate? time/space cost?
Empirical evaluation
run on test cases — normal input, boundary cases (empty, max size), invalid input
Comparing two algorithms
same correctness, different costs? pick the simpler one unless performance demands otherwise
assess: Given an algorithm for finding the max of a list, modify it to find the min instead
Design an algorithm to check if a word is a palindrome; test on 5 cases
Short answer: why test boundary cases even if the normal case works?

title: Python Data Types: int, float, string, bool, list
obj: Students will be able to declare and manipulate basic Python data types and predict the result of common type operations.
content: Integers and floats
int = whole numbers, float = decimals; arithmetic: + - * / // % **
Strings
text in quotes; concatenation with +; indexing with []; f-strings for formatting
Booleans
True and False; comparison operators ==, !=, <, >, <=, >=
Lists
ordered, mutable collection in [ ]; index access, .append(), .pop(), slicing
Type conversion
int('5'), str(42), float('3.14'), bool(0); when implicit coercion happens
type() and common errors
type(x) to inspect; TypeError when you mix incompatible types
assess: Predict the output: `'3' + str(5)` and `3 + int('5')`; explain the difference
Lab: write 5 lines of Python that use each data type at least once
Debug: given code with a TypeError, identify and fix the line

title: Python Control Flow: if, for, while
obj: Students will be able to write Python programs using conditionals, for-loops, and while-loops to control program execution.
content: if / elif / else
evaluate a boolean condition; branch to the matching block; else is the fallback
for loops
iterate over a sequence: `for x in [1,2,3]:` or `for i in range(10):`
while loops
repeat while a condition remains True; watch for infinite loops; break to exit early
Nested control flow
loops inside loops, or if inside for — used for grids, retries, multi-step logic
Common patterns
accumulator (running total), counter, filter, early-exit with break/continue
Event-driven basics
programs that wait for input and react — a loop + an if is the core pattern
assess: Write a program that asks for 5 numbers and prints the sum and average
Write a program that counts how many vowels are in a user-entered string
Debug: a while loop runs forever — identify why and fix

title: User Input, Sensor Input, and Decisions
obj: Students will be able to write a program that accepts user or sensor input and uses it to make decisions.
content: input() from the keyboard
reads a string; convert with int() or float() if you need a number
Validating input
check range, type, emptiness; loop until valid input arrives
Sensor input (intro)
on a Raspberry Pi or Arduino, sensors return numbers over time; process similarly to user input
Making decisions
chain if/elif on the input value; for sensors often compare to a threshold
Giving feedback
print meaningful messages; never just `print(x)` — label what the user is seeing
assess: Lab: write a program that asks for a temperature and outputs 'freezing', 'cold', 'warm', or 'hot'
Lab: write a program that accepts 3 test scores and outputs a letter grade
Design: describe how the same logic would work with a temperature sensor replacing user input

title: Organizing Data with Lists, Dicts, and Files
obj: Students will be able to write programs that collect different kinds of data and store them in appropriate Python structures.
content: Lists for ordered data
list of temperature readings, list of student names; append, index, iterate
Dictionaries for labeled data
`{'name': 'Ada', 'score': 92}` — key-value pairs when order matters less than labels
Lists of dicts
the most common shape for 'rows of records'; foundational for CSV/dataset work
Reading a CSV
`import csv; csv.DictReader(open(path))` → iterate rows as dicts
Writing output
print, write to a .txt or .csv file; f-strings for formatting
assess: Lab: build a list of 5 dicts representing students {name, score} and compute the average score
Lab: read a CSV file and print the 3rd column of each row
Short answer: when would you choose a list of dicts over a flat list?

title: Debugging and Tracing Code
obj: Students will be able to trace code execution by hand, identify common bug patterns, and use print-debugging or a debugger to isolate errors.
content: Error types
SyntaxError (code won't run), TypeError/ValueError (runtime), logic errors (wrong output, no crash)
Reading a traceback
bottom line names the error; follow the arrows back up to find your own code
Print-debugging
add print() at entry/exit of suspicious blocks; print variable values; remove when fixed
Code tracing by hand
walk through line-by-line with a variable table; catches logic errors before you run
Using a debugger
VS Code / PyCharm breakpoints; step over / into / out; watch expressions
Rubber-ducking
explain your code to a toy or peer — often reveals the bug yourself
assess: Trace by hand: given a loop that's supposed to sum odd numbers 1-10 but outputs wrong, find the bug
Lab: add 3 strategic print() statements to debug a broken function
Reflection: describe a bug you found and fixed — how did you localize it?

title: Comments, Documentation, and Code UX
obj: Students will be able to write useful comments and docstrings, follow naming conventions, and consider user experience when designing program output.
content: When to comment
explain WHY, not WHAT; flag gotchas; document unusual decisions; don't restate obvious code
Docstrings
the first string inside a function — describes purpose, args, return; readable with help()
Naming conventions
snake_case for variables/functions, PascalCase for classes; descriptive > short
User experience in CLI programs
label output, format numbers, handle errors gracefully, don't crash on empty input
Readability
short functions, consistent indentation, no magic numbers (use named constants)
assess: Rewrite a given 20-line function to add docstring, useful comments, and better names
Critique: given a CLI program's output, suggest 3 UX improvements
Short answer: when is a comment harmful rather than helpful?

title: Programming Paradigms: Procedural, OOP, Functional, Logic
obj: Students will be able to define the major programming paradigms and identify which paradigm a given code snippet exemplifies.
content: Procedural
step-by-step instructions grouped into functions; classic C-style; still how most simple scripts read
Object-oriented (OOP)
bundle data + behavior into objects; classes, inheritance; natural for modeling real things
Functional
compose pure functions, avoid mutation; map/filter/reduce; popular in data pipelines (Pandas chain calls)
Logic
declare facts and rules, let the system infer answers (Prolog); used in expert systems
Python is multi-paradigm
supports all four; you'll mostly mix procedural + OOP + functional in AI/DS code
assess: Classify 5 short snippets as procedural / OOP / functional / logic
Research: find a real ML library and identify which paradigms it uses
Short answer: why is 'multi-paradigm' an advantage for Python in data science?
